coredata.dc:title: Chitosan/carbon quantum dot/aptamer complex as a potential anticancer drug delivery system towards the release of 5-fluorouracil
coredata.dc:creator: Zavareh, Hediyeh Sepahi
Pourmadadi, Mehrab
Moradi, Ali
Yazdian, Fatemeh
Omidi, Meisam
coredata.dc:description: Nowadays, nanotechnology contributes diminishing side effects rather than traditional therapeutic methods like chemotherapy. Thus, designing a biocompatible specific targeted nanocarrier with prolonged half-life and enhanced bio-availability using simultaneous cell imaging seems urgent. To meet this demand, 5-fluorouracil-chitosan‑carbon quantum dot-aptamer (5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt) nanoparticle was successfully synthesized for specific targeted delivery of 5-FU anti-cancer drug used in breast cancer treatment and this was done by following facile water-in-oil (W/O) emulsification method. Physicochemical properties were characterized and high drug loading and entrapment efficiency were achieved. The average size and zeta potential of the nanoparticle were 122.7 nm and + 31.2 mV, respectively. According to the in-vitro drug release profile, 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt released the drug in a controlled manner. MTT assay, flow cytometry, fluorescence microscopy, and gene expression results demonstrated that the blank nanoparticle was biocompatible, and 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt could kill tumor cells efficiently. Bcl-2/Bax ratio was decreased after 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt treatment in MCF-7 cells. It was concluded that 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt could be used as a potential nanocarrier in breast cancer treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
Carbon quantum dot
Aptamer
Drug delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of drug loading and encapsulation efficiency

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Faculty of Medicine, Shahid Sadoughi University of Medical Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors would like to acknowledge Department of Clinical Biochemistry, Faculty of Medicine, Shahid Sadoughi University of Medical Sciences (Yazd, Iran) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of chitosan (a) and 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt (b) nanoparticles.
The 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt nanoparticles mean size measured via dynamic light scattering analysis (a); Visible blue emission of CQDs (b) under UV (365 nm).
FTIR spectra of CS-CQD, 5-FU-CS-CQD and 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt. All the samples were analyzed in the range between 4000 and 400 cm−1.
X-ray diffraction patterns of CS-CQD, 5-FU-CS-CQD and 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt.
In vitro release profile of 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt nanoparticles in pH 5.4 and pH 7.4 at 37 °C on predetermined time intervals by using dialysis bag method.

The viability analysis of MCF-7 cells treated with CS-CQD, 5-FU-CS-CQD, 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt and free 5-FU groups after 48 h. All the tests were done in triplicate and relative to the control group adjusted at 100% (*** signifies p-value≤0.001 in comparison with control).
Flow cytometry analyses of MCF-7 cells treated with control (a), CS-CQD (b), 5-FU-CS-CQD (c), 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt (d), and free 5-FU (e) for 48 h.

Fluorescent microscopy images of MCF-7 cells. (a), (c), and (e) respectively represent control, CS-CQD and 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt treated groups after 24 h; While (b), (d), and (f) show control, CS-CQD, and 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt treated groups after 48 h.

Gene expression analysis of Bcl-2 (a), Bax (b), and Bcl-2/Bax ratio (c). Data indicated that the Bcl-2/Bax ratio was decreased after treatment with 5-FU-CS-CQD and 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt significantly (*, ** and *** signifies p-value <0.01, p-value <0.001 and p-value <0.0001, respectively). 18 s gene was used as an internal control.
Comparison of EE% of 5-FU in different nanocarriers.

head.ce:title: Chitosan/carbon quantum dot/aptamer complex as a potential anticancer drug delivery system towards the release of 5-fluorouracil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Biochemistry, Faculty of Medicine, Shahid Sadoughi University of Medical Sciences, Yazd, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shahid Sadoughi University of Medical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Clinical Biochemistry, Faculty of Medicine, Shahid Sadoughi University of Medical Sciences, Yazd, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Life Science Engineering, Faculty of New Science and Technology, University of Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Life Science Engineering, Faculty of New Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Life Science Engineering, Faculty of New Science and Technology, University of Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Protein Research Center, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, GC 1983963113, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Protein Research Center, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, GC, 1983963113, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nowadays, nanotechnology contributes diminishing side effects rather than traditional therapeutic methods like chemotherapy. Thus, designing a biocompatible specific targeted nanocarrier with prolonged half-life and enhanced bio-availability using simultaneous cell imaging seems urgent. To meet this demand, 5-fluorouracil-chitosan‑carbon quantum dot-aptamer (5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt) nanoparticle was successfully synthesized for specific targeted delivery of 5-FU anti-cancer drug used in breast cancer treatment and this was done by following facile water-in-oil (W/O) emulsification method. Physicochemical properties were characterized and high drug loading and entrapment efficiency were achieved. The average size and zeta potential of the nanoparticle were 122.7 nm and + 31.2 mV, respectively. According to the in-vitro drug release profile, 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt released the drug in a controlled manner. MTT assay, flow cytometry, fluorescence microscopy, and gene expression results demonstrated that the blank nanoparticle was biocompatible, and 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt could kill tumor cells efficiently. Bcl-2/Bax ratio was decreased after 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt treatment in MCF-7 cells. It was concluded that 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt could be used as a potential nanocarrier in breast cancer treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Breast cancer is the most prevalent type of cancer among women. In 2018, more than two million women were afflicted by this type of cancer [1]. Although chemotherapy is a valuable method in cancer treatment, it causes various side effects following the metabolism of the anti-cancer drug by normal cells in addition to cancer cells; Hence low dosage of the drug reaches the cancer cells [2]. In addition to above, low drug loading efficiency and uncontrolled drug distribution, are other drawbacks of chemotherapy which limit its application. Nanotechnology enables researchers to design nanocarriers with specific targeted and sustained drug release capacity that can overcome shortcomings of chemotherapy [3,4]. Using nanoparticles may diminish the adverse effects in the pathway before reaching the goal organs [5,6].

5-FU is a chemotherapeutic drug that is administered to different types of cancers such as breast, colorectal and gastric cancers [7]. It leads to a wide variety of side effects like diarrhea, nausea, anemia, and neural or cardiac cytotoxicity [8]. 5-FU has a weak solubility with low stability in water [9]. This drug has a short half-life of about 15–20 min [8]. Nano drug delivery can increase drug half-life in the bloodstream [10].

Chitosan (CS) is an ample bio-polymer with outstanding characteristics such as appropriate biocompatibility, biodegradability, low immunogenicity, and efflux pump inhibition. Furthermore, it is more permeable to cells [11,12]. CS can be incorporated as a part of films, sponges, microspheres, nanospheres, and nanoparticles [13]. CS is pH-sensitive due to having amine groups [14]. These characteristics propose CS as a potential biopolymer to be applied in nano-drug delivery. Studies have shown that CS nanocarrier may decrease the side effects of 5-FU [15].

CQDs have shown attractive properties of appropriate photo-stability, water solubility, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and also being eco-friendly [16,17]. Hence, CQDs are applicable in drug delivery, wound healing [18], and also as a mitochondrial tracker [19]. Studies have shown that CQDs include NH, C-OH/C-O-C, and COOH groups [20] which enable them to interact with other functional groups. Quantum dots were also applied for 5-FU detection [21].

There are various nanodrug delivery systems such as liposomes, solid lipid nanoparticles, polymer nanoparticles, polymer micelles, dendrimers, polymersomes, hydrogel nanoparticles, and etc. that are used in cancer treatment. Among them, polymer nanoparticles are the most noticeable nanocarriers. The advantages of polymer nanoparticles are high stability, solubility, capacity for drug loading, the potential for specific targeting, and cancer cell imaging [22].

Yang et al. synthesized CQDs nanoparticles for Doxorubicin drug delivery towards MCF-7 cells. The nanoparticle had loaded the drug efficiently and was able to image the tumor cells simultaneously owing to the fluorescence property of CQDs [23].

Aptamers are single-stranded DNA or RNA oligonucleotides that are designed for a specific target molecule with high affinity and specificity [24]. MUC-1 is a glycoprotein which exists in the membrane of all the cells. In some types of cancers like breast cancer, this glycoprotein is under-glycosylated and highly overexpressed. 5TR1 aptamer can be attached to this glycoprotein specifically and get inside the cell via endocytosis [25].

CQDs were used for dual drug delivery of 5-FU and Glu specifically by Gui and co-workers. The drugs were delivered specific-targeted due to the presence of a specific aptamer for alpha fetal protein (AFP). After AFP binding to its aptamer, 5-FU was released successfully [26]. In other study, Jin et al. developed a novel probe for the detection of CA125 antigen instead of traditional assays. 5-FU as a quencher was attached to the aptamer of CA125 via electrostatic interactions [27].

Our present work aimed at constructing a novel specific targeted nanoparticle with sustainable drug release profile which could diminish the drug side effects and increase its half-life and bioavailability. To reach this goal, we designed 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt in which the passive and active targeting approaches were adopted together in one system to minimize 5-FU vast side effects and simultaneously imaging the cells with the help of fluorescent carbon quantum dots. To the best of our knowledge, 5-fluorouracil loaded chitosan carbon quantum dot decorated with 5TR1 aptamer had not been synthesized before.

Low molecular weight chitosan, di-ammonium hydrogen citrate, DMEM/high glucose, FBS, trypsin/EDTA 0.25%, penicillin/streptomycin, Dialysis bag (12kD cut off) and MTT powder were purchased from Sigma Aldrich Chemical Co., 5-fluorouracil was purchased from EBEWE Pharma (Austria), 5TR1 aptamer with the sequence of 5-GAAGTGAAAATGACAGAACACAACA-3 was purchased from Yahooteb Novin Co.

Firstly, 0.4 g of powdered chitosan was dissolved in 19.6 mL of (2% w/v) 2% acetic acid (0.4 mL of acetic acid was dispersed in 19.6 mL of distilled water). Then it was stirred on a magnetic stirrer with moderate RPM to reach a homogenous solution. After that, 0.2 mL of CQD solution (1% v/v) was added and it was left to be mixed well for 30 min on a magnetic stirrer.
5-FU was used as a model drug. 2% (v/v) of 5-FU was added to the above solution and it was given 15 min to be blended completely on a magnetic stirrer.

4 μL aptamer solution with a concentration of 100 μM was added into the aforementioned solution (0.02% v/v) and it was stirred for 15 min. Then it was incubated for 2 h at room temperature. The resultant solution as a hydrophilic phase was dripped into a beaker containing 0.4 mL span 80 and 19.6 mL olive oil (2% v/v span 80 to olive oil) and then it was stirred on a magnetic stirrer with a moderate RPM to form a milky water-in-oil emulsion. Then, 15 mL PVA and 25 mL of distilled water were poured into the emulsion and this resulted in sedimentation of the particles at the bottom of the beaker. Then, the hydrophobic phase on top was discarded and the hydrophilic phase was centrifuged with 3500 ×g for 10 min in order to wash and remove the remnants of hydrophobic and hydrophilic emulsifiers and also to obtain a clear and pure nanoparticle.

The image of 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt was taken using a scanning electron microscope. The particle size and zeta potential of 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt were measured by dynamic light scattering using a Zetasizer Nano-ZS at 25 °C. All the measurements were performed in triplicate. FTIR was done to identify the formation of CS-CQD, 5-FU-CS-CQD, and 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt nanoparticles and to determine the CQD and CS functional groups. All the samples were analyzed in the range between 4000 and 400 cm−1. The crystalline nature of CS-CQD, 5-FU-CS-CQD, and 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt were compared by powder XRD patterns using an X-ray diffractometer at room temperature [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of drug loading and encapsulation efficiency
ce:section.ce:para:
To determine drug loading and entrapment efficiencies, 1 mL of ethyl acetate was added to 1 mL of 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt. The mixture was shaken well and the ethyl acetate phase was separated. The amount of free 5-FU in the ethyl acetate phase was measured by a UV–visible spectrophotometer at 266 nm [29]. The percentage of 5-FU entrapment efficiency (EE%) and loading efficiency was computed using the equations given below:
Entrapment Efficiency
%
=
Total amount of
5 − FU
−
Free amount of
5 − FU
Total amount of
5 − FU
× 100
Loading Efficiency
%
=
Total amount of
5 − FU
−
Free amount of
5 − FU
Total amount of Nanoparticle
× 100

The release of 5-FU from 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt nanoparticle was assessed using a 12 kD Mw cut-off dialysis bag in two different media; An acidic medium which was made from acetic acid solved in PBS adjusted to pH 5.4 and a physiologic medium which was made of PBS with pH 7.4 to mimic the usual conditions of tumor micro-environment and normal pH of the blood, respectively; Then it was put into a beaker containing water with a temperature of 37 °C, and then was stirred continuously on a magnetic stirrer. 1 mL of 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt solution was transferred into a dialysis bag and was immersed in two 15 mL falcons containing acidic and normal media separately. At predetermined time intervals of 10 min, 20 min, 30 min, 1 h, 2 h, 3 h, 6 h, 12 h, 24 h, 48 h, 72 h, and 96 h, 300 μL of samples was elicited and replaced with an equivalent volume of the same fresh medium. UV-optical density was measured by UV–Visible spectrophotometer at 266 nm. Using a standard curve for 5-FU, the cumulative release percentage of the drug was calculated. The amount of released 5-FU was estimated using the following equation:
5 − FU
Released
%
=
5 − FU
released
5 − FU
loaded
× 100

The 5-FU release performance was also evaluated at different pH values (6.0, 6.5, 7.0, 8.0), different temperatures (20 °C, 25 °C, 37 °C, 40 °C, 45 °C) and different NaCl salt concentrations (0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5 mol/L).

MCF-7 breast cancer cell line was purchased from the Pasteur Institute of Iran. The MCF-7 cells were cultured in high glucose DMEM medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin-streptomycin at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2.

3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was performed to analyze the cytotoxicity of CS-CQD, 5-FU-CS-CQD, 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt, and free drug. An equivalent drug concentration of 4.8 μg/mL was used in all the drug-containing formulations. MCF-7 cells were seeded at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well in a 96-well microplate containing 200 μL DMEM medium and then they were incubated for 24 h in order to let the cells attach with a confluency over 80%. After 24 h, the cells were treated with the above groups and incubated for 48 h. The cells which received no treatment were considered as control. After 48 h, 20 μL of MTT reagent with the concentration of 5 mg/mL in PBS (pH 7.4) was added to each well to be incubated for 4 h at 37 °C. Subsequently, the medium was discarded and replaced with 200 μL DMSO to dissolve formazan crystals. After a gentle shaking of about 10 min, the absorbance of each well was detected in 570 nm wavelength using a microplate reader. Cell viability percentage was computed using the below equation:
Cell viability
%
=
A
sample
A
control
× 100
[A]sample is the absorbance of cells tested with different formulations and [A]control is the absorbance of cells without receiving any treatment.

MCF-7 cells were seeded in a six-well plate at a density of 3 × 105 cells per well and incubated for 24 h. Then, the cells were treated with different formulations of CS-CQD, 5-FU-CS-CQD, 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt, and free drug with an equivalent drug concentration of 4.8 μg/mL and they were all incubated for 48 h. The cells which received no treatment were considered as control. Subsequently, the cells were trypsinized and harvested, then washed with PBS and suspended in 100 μL binding buffer. All the cells were stained with Propidium Iodide and Annexin V-fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC), placed in the dark for 15 min, and finally analyzed by a flow cytometer (BD Biosciences, USA).

The cellular uptake of CS-CQD and 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt by MCF-7 cells was examined using fluorescence microscopy (OPTIKA). At a density of 2 × 105 per well, MCF-7 cells were seeded on 6-well plates and incubated for 24 h. Afterwards, the cells were treated with CS-CQD and 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt for 24 h and 48 h (The drug concentration was 4.8 μg/mL). Subsequently, they were washed three times with PBS and fixed with 2% paraformaldehyde for 15 min followed by washing with PBS and analyzing with fluorescence microscopy.

MCF-7 cells were cultured in a six-well plate for 24 h, then treated with CS-CQD, 5-FU-CS-CQD, 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt and free drug for 48 h. The drug concentration was equivalent in all the drug-containing groups (4.8 μg/mL). The total RNA was isolated from the MCF-7 cells using BON RNA lysis buffer by following the manufacturer's instruction. In the next step, cDNA was transcribed using the YTA cDNA synthesis kit. Real-Time PCR amplification was done using YTA SYBR Green qPCR mastermix 2× for 40 cycles according to the following protocol: 95 °C for 10 min, 95 °C for 10 s, 60 °C for 40 s and ramp 55 °C to 95 °C, rinsing at 0.5 °C on each step. 18 s gene was considered as the internal control. The sequences of the primers were as follows: Bcl-2 forward 5-GATAACGGAGGCTGGGATG-3 and reverse 5-CAGGAGAAATCAAACAGAGGC-3, Bax forward 5-CAAACTGGTGCTCAAGGC-3 and reverse 5-CACAAAGATGGTCACGGTC-3, 18 s forward 5-AGGAATTCCCAGTAAGTGC <G > -3 and reverse 5-GCCTCACTAAACCATCCA <A > -3.
The experimental values were reported as mean ± SD. The statistical analysis was performed using one-way ANOVA with the student's t-test. P-value ≤0.05 was considered significant.

CQDs were synthesized facilely by following the hydrothermal method. 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt was synthesized with the assistance of a facile and cost-effective W/O emulsification method. Polyvinyl Alcohol (PVA) is a hydrophilic substance with a suitable emulsifying capacity and it was used in the process of 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt nanoparticle synthesis. PVA is biodegradable due to the hydrolysis of its hydroxyl groups [25]. Span 80 was used as hydrophobic emulsifier and instead of using liquid paraffin which was applied in former works; olive oil was used for the first time as a natural and biocompatible hydrophobic phase in order to stabilize the nanoparticle.

CQDs as a biocompatible carbon nanomaterial were used in 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt structure for better drug release and cell imaging. Since CQDs give a negative charge to the nanoparticle, we combine it with chitosan which is a safe bio-polymer and can interact with CQDs electrostatically and also give the nanoparticle positive charge and high stability for better interaction with cell surfaces accompanied with pH-sensitivity feature for passive targeting. 5TR1 25-mer aptamer with high affinity and specificity for MUC-1 overexpressed glycoprotein on MCF-7 cell membranes was chosen to make the nanoparticle specific targeted and to improve its uptaking by the cells.
SEM images were indicated that CQDs had a spherical shape and were monodispersed. The rough surface of nanoparticle was due to the presence of drug in it [3].

The mean size of 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt was estimated at 122.7 nm. Nanoparticles with a size less than 200 nm were successfully distributed inside capillaries without any problem [30]. They could also reach the tumor site following the EPR (Enhanced Permeability and Retention) effect [31]. Zeta potential of 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt was estimated at about +31.2 mV. High zeta potential was correlated with more stability and repellence between the nanoparticle components [32]. The positive charge of the nanoparticle could be related to the positive amine groups of chitosan structure. The zeta potential of the nanoparticle was appropriate and it could be concluded that the nanoparticle was stable. It was proved that the positive surface charge of the nanoparticle had the advantage of better uptaking by the cells [15]. Therefore the nanoparticle electrostatically interacted with cell surfaces.

The FTIR spectrum showed C-OH stretching in the range of 1000–1200 cm−1 and CC stretching band in the structure of CQDs at 1537 cm−1 [33]. The peak at 1550 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrational band of NH which demonstrated deacetylated amine groups of chitosan [34]. The peak around 1400 cm−1 corresponded to CO and/or CN bending which was attributed to the presence of amine groups. The bands between 2800 and 3000 cm−1 demonstrated asymmetric and symmetric CH aliphatic stretching that showed the stability of nanoparticle in the aqueous medium [35]. The peaks at 1150.02, 1248.94, and 1689.89 cm−1 were related to CF, CN and CO, respectively which were in line with Yan et al. results and approved that 5-FU was loaded successfully [36].

XRD was done to further approve the nanoparticle formation. The peak at 2θ = 18 ͦ was due to high crystallinity of chitosan [37]. The presence of a wide peak in 2θ = 23 ͦ was related to the amorphous structure of carbon in carbon quantum dot [38]. A sharp peak at 2θ = 28.5 ͦ was due to the existence of 5-FU that was successfully loaded into nanoparticle structure [39].

In the process of nanoparticle synthesis, olive oil was used for the first time as a natural substance instead of liquid paraffin in other works [40]. Entrapment efficiency and drug loading percentages were calculated 84.7 ± 5.5% and 37.2 ± 3.8%, respectively. High entrapment efficiency and drug loading percentages were obtained and it's all compared with other nanoparticles in Table 1 . A high percentage of drug loading is related to the porous feature of the nanoparticle which has the ability to trap more drug molecules inside it.

Jin et al. prepared a light dual-sensitive core/shell hybrid nanosphere as a smart carrier for pH and photothermal induced 5-FU release. Its average size was measured 195 nm using DLS. The drug loading and EE% related to 5-FU concentration were 1.6–52.5% and 16.4–66.5%, respectively. The cell viability for carrier +5-FU was more than free 5-FU and this was attributed to the high efficiency of the nanocarrier in pH-responsive 5-FU delivery [41].

Hua and co-workers used CQDs for PpIX (protoporphyrin photosensitizer) nucleolus targeted delivery in photodynamic therapy. PpIX loaded CQDs had a drug loading percentage of 23.3% and could successfully image the cells [42].

Guo et al. synthesized a biocompatible chitosan‑carbon nanocage hybrid loaded with 5-FU for sustained drug release and co-irradiation induced cancer treatment. The nanoparticle had an average DLS size around 960.8 ± 184.6 nm, loading efficiency of about 22.4 ± 2.0% and could induce cell apoptosis [43].

Comparing to above-mentioned studies, 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt has appropriate size and drug loading efficiency. (See Figs. 1–4
.)

The drug release pattern was investigated in pH 5.4 and pH 7.4 in Fig. 5 ; these two pHs were chosen to mimic the usual pH of the tumor micro-environment and normal pH of the blood, respectively. As Fig. 5 depicts, a rapid release occurred between 10 min to 6 h, then a slow release was observed between 6 h and 48 h and continued by a constant release rate. According to Fig. 5, after 24 h, 71% of the drug was released in pH 5.4, but 77% of the drug was retained inside the nanoparticle in pH 7.4. After 48 h, the drug was almost entirely released in pH 5.4 while nearly half of the drug still remained inside the nanoparticle in pH 7.4. The trend of drug release was slower in pH 7.4 and the drug was released faster from 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt in pH 5.4 which indicates pH sensitivity of the nanoparticle and better drug release in tumor micro-environment and the diminishing of drug side effects. In acidic pH, the amine groups are protonated and repelling each other. This protonation of amine and carboxyl groups changes the electrostatic interaction between chitosan and carbon quantum dot, which contributes to drug release.

5-FU release rate was evaluated at different pH values (6.0, 6.5, 7.0, 8.0), different temperatures (20 °C, 25 °C, 37 °C, 40 °C, 45 °C) and different NaCl salt concentrations (0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5 mol/L). The drug was released with a lower rate owing to less protonation of amine groups when the pH and the NaCl salt concentration were increased from 6.0 to 8.0 and 0.01 mol/L to 0.5 mol/L, respectively; The 5-FU release rate was faster as the temperature was increased from 20 °C to 45 °C following the faster molecular motion.

Nejadshafiee et al. observed that the release of 5-FU in acidic pH could be due to the sensitivity of chitosan to this pH [12]. Malekimusavi and co-workers investigated 5-fluorouracil release from Graphene oxide-L-arginine nanogel. Their results showed that the drug was released faster in acidic pH than physiologic pH which is consistent with our findings [44]. Faster 5-FU release in acidic pH occurred as a result of more sensitivity of the nanoparticle structure to pH changes. On the other hand, the amine groups of chitosan were charged positively thus repelling each other, and consequently, this let the drug to be released [3]. The protonation of amine and carboxyl groups destroyed the electrostatic interaction between chitosan and CQD.

Cytotoxicity of CS-CQD, 5-FU-CS-CQD, 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt, and free drug on MCF-7 cells was determined via MTT assay. Untreated MCF-7 cells were employed as a control group. After 48 h, the blank nanoparticle did not show any toxicity against MCF-7 cells. The cell viability of 5-FU-CS-CQD was calculated about 67% which demonstrates its significant toxicity towards cancer cells. Aptamer caused nearly 20% more toxicity in 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt in comparison with 5-FU-CS-CQD [45]. The drug concentration in all of the groups was 4.8 μg/mL.

MCF-7 cell line was chosen for MUC-1 overexpression on its surface. According to cell cytotoxicity results, the blank nanoparticle was biocompatible because it showed no cytotoxicity in MCF-7 cells. 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt resulted in a significant cell death in MCF-7 cell line. It could be concluded that aptamer improved the killing ability of the nanoparticle against cancer cells, so 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt can be used as an efficient nanocarrier in other MUC-1 over-expressing cancers. Although the cell viability of 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt was approximately the same as the free drug, it should be noticed that at the same time, the amount of released drug was less than free 5-FU owing to sustained drug release [46]. In other words, delivering the drug by nanoparticle might lessen its drug waste and consequent side effects. Our results were in line with Javanbakht and co-workers' MTT assay results [47]. Also, Pina et al. used a 5TR1 aptamer in active targeting drug delivery, and they demonstrated that it was lead to 20% more cell death compared with using nanocarrier without aptamer [45]. Moosavian and co-workers also found out that using 5TR1 aptamer made a significant change in cell death in comparison with nanocarrier without aptamer [25]. (See Fig. 6 .)

Flow cytometry was done to determine the percentages of cell apoptosis and cell necrosis. The quadrant regions are shown in the flow cytometry chart, in which Q1, Q2, Q3 and Q4 are related to necrotic or necroptotic (FITC−/PI+), late apoptotic (FITC+/PI+), early apoptotic (FITC+/PI−), and alive cells (FITC−/PI−), respectively. Flow cytometry results are represented in Fig. 7 ; as it shows, after 48 h treatment of MCF-7 cells, the cell viability percentage of CS-CQD nanoparticle treated group was not changed significantly in comparison with the control group, and this approves the biocompatibility of the nanoparticle. 5-FU-CS-CQD could initiate apoptosis effectively. Among all the groups, 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt caused the most cell death in which only 60% of the MCF-7 cells remained alive after treatment. The group that was treated with 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt had a higher cell death rate in comparison with the 5-FU-CS-CQD group and this demonstrates the efficient role of aptamer in better uptaking by cells and moreover it is in line with the results of Rață et al. [48]. Therefore, 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt functioned better than free 5-FU in MCF-7 cells elimination. In another research by Zhan and co-workers, approximately 35% necrosis resulted from the effect of a nanocarrier containing 5-FU drug and AS1411 aptamer on MCF-7 cells [49].

CQDs showed the most absorbance at around 320 nm via UV–Visible spectroscopy and emitted blue light under UV. Owing to the optical imaging property of CQDs and by means of fluorescence microscopy 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt was traced inside the MCF-7 cells. As Fig. 8 depicts, (a) and (b) are related to the controls with no treatment, while (c) and (d) are the groups that were treated with the nanoparticles without drug, and (e) and (f) are the groups that were treated with the drug-loaded nanoparticles after 24 h and 48 h, respectively. According to Fig. 8, compared with (e) and (f), the fluorescence intensity was higher in (c) and (d) which confirms the biocompatibility of the nanoparticles. When the cells were treated with the 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt, the fluorescence intensity was decreased which shows its efficient capacity for eliminating cancer cells. Compared to (c), enhanced fluorescence intensity in (d) was due to an increase in uptake of the nanoparticles inside the cytoplasm of the cells over time. On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity was decreased in (f) rather to (e) as a consequence of more cell death during time. (See Fig. 9 .)

Some members of Bcl-2 family proteins could result in apoptosis and some could inhibit it [50]. To assess the effect of CS-CQD, 5-FU-CS-CQD, 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt, and free drug on gene expression of apoptotic (Bax) and anti-apoptotic (Bcl-2) genes, Real-Time PCR was conducted. The cells without receiving any treatment were used as control. Bcl-2/Bax ratio is a key factor in the determination of apoptosis. A decrease in the Bcl-2/Bax ratio could result in apoptosis. Our findings indicate that the Bcl-2/Bax ratio was decreased after treating MCF-7 cells with 5-FU-CS-CQD and 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt significantly. Bcl-2/Bax ratio for 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt was decreased more compared with the 5-FU-CS-CQD group. Our results were in agreement with Wang et al. findings in which they announced the group that was treated with 5-FU nanoparticle decreased Bcl-2 and increased Bax mRNA level expression in comparison with control group [51]. Mohiyudin and co-workers also found out that after the 5-fluorouracil-loaded calcium phosphate nanoparticles treatment, Bcl-2 was down-regulated and Bax was up-regulated compared with the control group [52]. Cheng et al. also showed that Galactosylated chitosan/5-fluorouracil nanoparticles could diminish the Bcl-2/Bax ratio effectively [53].

In this study, 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt was synthesized successfully owing to W/O emulsification method. The nanoparticle formation was approved via FTIR and XRD analyses. Appropriate zeta potential and size accompanied with high drug loading and entrapment efficiency were all accomplished. Following the pH changes 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt showed a pH-sensitive and sustained drug release profile. Incorporation of aptamer on the surface of the nanoparticle helped it to be better uptaken by MCF-7 cells and caused more cytotoxicity. According to MTT, flow cytometry, fluorescence microscopy, and Real-Time PCR test results, the blank nanoparticle was biocompatible and 5-FU-CS-CQD-Apt revealed effective cytotoxicity against MCF-7 cells thus could be applied as a potential nanocarrier in breast cancer treatment.
Hediyeh Sepahi Zavareh: Methodology, Investigation, Resources, Writing - Original Draft.
Mehrab Pourmadadi: Methodology, Validation.
Ali Moradi: Supervision, Conceptualization.
Fatemeh Yazdian: Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - Review & Editing.
Meisam Omidi: Conceptualization, Formal analysis

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors would like to acknowledge Department of Clinical Biochemistry, Faculty of Medicine, Shahid Sadoughi University of Medical Sciences (Yazd, Iran) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The protocol for CQDs synthesis is included as supplementary materials. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2020.09.166.

Carbohydr. Polym.

RSC Adv.
